name: Brute Force (Recursion)
summary: Try both moves at every stair: recurse to i+1 and i+2, counting paths that land exactly on n.
visualizationIntent: init: empty stage — no calls made yet. recurse: each dfs(i) call pops in a new tree node labelled with the stair index i and links under its parent (left = dfs(i+1), right = dfs(i+2)); the active call is the vermillion cursor, ancestors still on the stack are amber. leaf: a call that lands exactly on n turns green (result — a valid way, returns 1); a call that overshoots n turns red (error — dead end, returns 0). return: internal nodes turn blue (visited) as they sum their two children; the full tree shows repeated identical subtrees — the exponential recomputation that motivates DP. The running answer accumulates to the total number of green leaves.

lineExplanations.3: Start an empty list of recursion-tree nodes.
lineExplanations.4: Start a lookup from a node id to its node.
lineExplanations.5: Start a per-node state tracker (active / returned / leaf).
lineExplanations.6: Explore from stair 0 — this one call unfolds the whole tree.
lineExplanations.7: Return the total number of distinct ways.
lineExplanations.10: This call's node id is the next free slot.
lineExplanations.11: Create a node labelled with the current stair i.
lineExplanations.12: Add the node to the tree so it appears on screen.
lineExplanations.13: Register the node under its id for fast lookup.
lineExplanations.14: Mark this call active — its frame is on the call stack.
lineExplanations.15: Does this call have a parent? (the very first call does not.)
lineExplanations.16: Was this the parent's first move (climb 1 step)?
lineExplanations.17: Link this node as the parent's left child (the +1 branch).
lineExplanations.19: Link this node as the parent's right child (the +2 branch).
lineExplanations.20: Did we land exactly on the top stair?
lineExplanations.21: Tag this a success leaf — one complete valid way.
lineExplanations.22: Return 1: this path reaches the top exactly.
lineExplanations.23: Did we step past the top stair?
lineExplanations.24: Tag this a dead end — it overshot the top.
lineExplanations.25: Return 0: overshooting is not a valid climb.
lineExplanations.26: Try climbing 1 step: recurse into stair i+1.
lineExplanations.27: Try climbing 2 steps: recurse into stair i+2.
lineExplanations.28: Ways from here = ways via +1 plus ways via +2.
lineExplanations.29: Mark this call finished (it has returned).
lineExplanations.30: Return this stair's total to the caller.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution(object): the wrapper class LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def climbStairs(self, n): the entry method; n is the number of stairs.
syntaxExplanations.5: state_by_id = [] will hold one status number per node.
syntaxExplanations.6: self.dfs(0, ...) calls the recursive helper starting at stair 0.
syntaxExplanations.7: return answer hands back the count.
syntaxExplanations.9: def dfs(self, i, n, parent_id, slot, ...): the recursive helper; the extra args let the visualization see the tree each frame.
syntaxExplanations.10: len(nodes) is how many nodes exist — this call's id.
syntaxExplanations.11: A dict literal { ... } builds this call's node.
syntaxExplanations.12: nodes.append(node) adds it to the tree.
syntaxExplanations.13: by_id[my_id] = node registers it by id.
syntaxExplanations.15: is not None — the first call has no parent.
syntaxExplanations.17: by_id[parent_id]["left"] = my_id links to the parent.
syntaxExplanations.19: by_id[parent_id]["right"] = my_id links to the parent.
syntaxExplanations.20: if i == n: reached the top exactly.
syntaxExplanations.23: if i > n: stepped past the top.
syntaxExplanations.28: total = left_val + right_val sums both branches.
syntaxExplanations.30: return total passes the count up.
